title: William Neville Hart

infobox.value: An Act to enable William Nevil Blondeau Esquire and his. Issue to take and use the Surname of Hart.

(27 December 1741 in [St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace"), London – 23 October 1804, [Inveraray Castle](/wiki/Inveraray_Castle "Inveraray Castle"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")) was a British banker, politician and diplomat. He was born to Denise Gougeon, the wife of Lewis Augustus Blondeau. His mother was the Under Housekeeper or Mistress of the King's Household, [a position](/wiki/Royal_Households_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Households of the United Kingdom") she was to hold for more than fifty years.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Denise was the sister of Esther Gougeon, the wife of [Daniel Cornelius de Beaufort](/wiki/Daniel_Cornelius_de_Beaufort "Daniel Cornelius de Beaufort"). Hart's father held various positions at Court including that of Gentleman Usher to [King George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

([5 Geo. 3](/wiki/5_Geo._3 "5 Geo. 3"). c. *17* Pr.) of 22 March 1765.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Hart had married firstly on 7 January 1765 Elizabeth, daughter of Sir Cæsar Hawkins, 1st Baronet (1711–1786), serjeant-surgeon to the King, and grandfather of [Caesar Hawkins](/wiki/Caesar_Hawkins "Caesar Hawkins"), in turn serjeant-surgeon to [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom"). Elizabeth, unfortunately, died on 30 October 1766 *s.p.*

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British banker, politician and diplomat

**William Neville Hart** (27 December 1741 in [St James's Palace](/wiki/St_James%27s_Palace "St James's Palace"), London – 23 October 1804, [Inveraray Castle](/wiki/Inveraray_Castle "Inveraray Castle"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland")) was a British banker, politician and diplomat. He was born to Denise Gougeon, the wife of Lewis Augustus Blondeau. His mother was the Under Housekeeper or Mistress of the King's Household, [a position](/wiki/Royal_Households_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Households of the United Kingdom") she was to hold for more than fifty years.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Denise was the sister of Esther Gougeon, the wife of [Daniel Cornelius de Beaufort](/wiki/Daniel_Cornelius_de_Beaufort "Daniel Cornelius de Beaufort"). Hart's father held various positions at Court including that of Gentleman Usher to [King George II](/wiki/George_II_of_Great_Britain "George II of Great Britain").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Marriages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Neville_Hart&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Marriages")]

| Blondeau's Name Act 1765 | |
| --- | --- |
| Act of Parliament | |
| [coat of arms](/wiki/File:Coat_of_arms_of_Great_Britain_(1714%E2%80%931801).svg) [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") | |
| [Long title](/wiki/Short_and_long_titles "Short and long titles") | An Act to enable William Nevil Blondeau Esquire and his. Issue to take and use the Surname of Hart. |
| [Citation](/wiki/Citation_of_United_Kingdom_legislation "Citation of United Kingdom legislation") | [5 Geo. 3](/wiki/5_Geo._3 "5 Geo. 3"). c. *17* Pr. |
| Dates | |
| [Royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") | 22 March 1765 |

Following the death of his father, and the remarriage of his mother to Sir William Hart Kt., a banker and Sheriff of London, William Neville Blondeau took the surname of Hart by [private act of Parliament](/wiki/Private_act_of_Parliament "Private act of Parliament"), **Blondeau's Name Act 1765** ([5 Geo. 3](/wiki/5_Geo._3 "5 Geo. 3"). c. *17* Pr.) of 22 March 1765.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Hart had married firstly on 7 January 1765 Elizabeth, daughter of Sir Cæsar Hawkins, 1st Baronet (1711–1786), serjeant-surgeon to the King, and grandfather of [Caesar Hawkins](/wiki/Caesar_Hawkins "Caesar Hawkins"), in turn serjeant-surgeon to [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom"). Elizabeth, unfortunately, died on 30 October 1766 *s.p.*

He married secondly Elizabeth Aspinwall. Her father was [Stanhope Aspinwall](/wiki/Stanhope_Aspinwall "Stanhope Aspinwall"), who on his mother's side, was a great great-grandson of [Philip Stanhope, 1st Earl of Chesterfield](/wiki/Philip_Stanhope,_1st_Earl_of_Chesterfield "Philip Stanhope, 1st Earl of Chesterfield").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Aspinwall was a diplomat who had served in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") and [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers") and at the time of his death in 1771 was Secretary to [Earl Harcourt](/wiki/Earl_Harcourt "Earl Harcourt"), [Ambassador to France](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_Great_Britain_to_France "List of ambassadors of Great Britain to France"). Aspinwall's wife was named *Magdalena*, but little else appears to be known of her.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Career in England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Neville_Hart&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career in England")]

Hart entered the banking firm of Blackwell, Hart, Darrell, and Croft, of [Pall Mall](/wiki/Pall_Mall,_London "Pall Mall, London"). Hart entered the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain") for [Stafford](/wiki/Stafford_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Stafford (UK Parliament constituency)") on 12 April 1770, apparently without opposition, on what interest it is not known.[[7]](#cite_note-7) He took the degree of D.C.L. from [Oxford University](/wiki/Oxford_University "Oxford University") in 1772. In the House of Commons, Hart made seven speeches, one notably on the proposed [Royal Marriages Act 1772](/wiki/Royal_Marriages_Act_1772 "Royal Marriages Act 1772"); he spoke for the Court. Another dealt with the appointment of Oliver over the printer's case. He did not stand in 1774.

## The Continent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Neville_Hart&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The Continent")]

After leaving [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") in 1774, Hart travelled extensively on the Continent, France, Italy, Germany and the northern courts. He went to [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") where he became Chamberlain to [Stanislaus Augustus Poniatowski](/wiki/Stanislaus_Augustus_Poniatowski "Stanislaus Augustus Poniatowski"), the last king of Poland. On 27 December 1794, Hart was created knight of the [Order of Saint Stanislaus](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Stanislaus "Order of Saint Stanislaus"). King Stanislaus also conferred on him the [Order of the White Eagle](/wiki/Order_of_the_White_Eagle_(Poland) "Order of the White Eagle (Poland)").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Back in England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Neville_Hart&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Back in England")]

Hart returned to England the following year and in October received a letter from the [Duke of Portland](/wiki/William_Cavendish-Bentinck,_3rd_Duke_of_Portland "William Cavendish-Bentinck, 3rd Duke of Portland") to the effect that [King George III](/wiki/George_III_of_Great_Britain "George III of Great Britain") had authorised him wear the Orders given to him. Even though he was thereafter called Sir William Neville Hart, it is not clear whether the King's authorisation went as far as that. He also had the honour of kissing the hands of both the King and of [Queen Charlotte](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte "Queen Charlotte") as a mark of special favour.[[9]](#cite_note-9) While on the Continent, Hart had kept extensive journals, but they were destroyed with other possessions in the 1802 fire at Roseneath Castle, the seat of the [Duke of Argyll](/wiki/Duke_of_Argyll "Duke of Argyll").

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Neville_Hart&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Death")]

Hart's second wife, the daughter of Stanhope Aspinwall, apparently had died in 1783 and, according to *[The Gentleman's Magazine](/wiki/The_Gentleman%27s_Magazine "The Gentleman's Magazine")*, at *St Bemain de Colboe, [Normandy](/wiki/Normandy "Normandy")*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It is possible that it was in fact *[St-Romain-de-Colbosc](/wiki/St-Romain-de-Colbosc "St-Romain-de-Colbosc")* in Normandy.

The death certificate of Hart's wife, Elizabeth Aspinwall, can be found in the Archives of Seine Maritime. She died on 12 October 1783 in Gommerville, Seine Maritime, Normandy, where she was living at the time. She was in the 38th year of her life and died of a fever.;[[11]](#cite_note-11) her tombstone can be seen in the park around the castle of Filières with the epitaph : ici repose sous la garde de l'amitié Elisabeth Aspinwal mariée à Guillaume Nevil Hart membre du parlement d'Angleterre morte à 34 ans le 12 octobre 1783 également distinguée par son esprit et sa beauté des Français ont élevé ce monument à la mémoire de cette angloise ; a painting of Elisabeth Aspinwal can be seen in the castle of Filières

On 23 October 1804, Hart died at [Inveraray Castle](/wiki/Inveraray_Castle "Inveraray Castle"), owned by the Duke of Argyll,

## Children and issue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Neville_Hart&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Children and issue")]

Hart and Elizabeth, the daughter of Stanhope Aspinwall, had two sons and four daughters:[[12]](#cite_note-12)-

* William Stanhope Hart, born 17 October 1769 and baptised 14 November at [St James's Church, Piccadilly](/wiki/St_James%27s_Church,_Piccadilly "St James's Church, Piccadilly"), died in his father's lifetime.
* William Neville Hart, sometime Captain in the then 79th Cameron Highlanders, was born 19 July 1772 and baptised 12 August also at St James's Church, Piccadilly. He was the father of Reverend Henry Cornelius Hart, presumably the *H. C. Hart* mentioned in the *References*.
* Elizabeth Hart, born 28 August 1768 and baptised 28 September at St James Church, Piccadilly, married on 4 June 1787 [Dr John Griffiths](/wiki/John_Griffiths_(surgeon) "John Griffiths (surgeon)"), surgeon to [Queen Charlotte](/wiki/Queen_Charlotte "Queen Charlotte")'s Household from 1792 to 1818,[[13]](#cite_note-13) the parents of [George Richard Griffiths](/wiki/George_Richard_Griffiths "George Richard Griffiths")[[14]](#cite_note-14) and the grandparents of [George Neville Griffiths](/wiki/George_Griffiths_(Australian_politician) "George Griffiths (Australian politician)") M.L.A.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The latter was the grandfather of [William Charles Wentworth](/wiki/William_Wentworth_(Australian_politician) "William Wentworth (Australian politician)") M.P. (1907–2003).
* Louisa Alexandrina Hart, born 20 December 1770 and baptised 7 January 1771 at St James Church, Piccadilly, married Lieutenant–Colonel George Lyon. Their elder son, Captain [George Francis Lyon](/wiki/George_Francis_Lyon "George Francis Lyon") R.N., the Arctic and African explorer in turn married Lucy Louisa, younger daughter of the Irish revolutionary, [Lord Edward Fitzgerald](/wiki/Lord_Edward_Fitzgerald "Lord Edward Fitzgerald").
* Sophia Hart, born 11 November 1773 and baptised 13 December, married 14 December 1795 Richard Newton Bennett.
* Caroline Frances Hart, born 1 February 1775t, married Dr Griffiths' younger brother, [Lieutenant–General Charles Griffiths](/wiki/Charles_Griffiths_(British_Army_officer) "Charles Griffiths (British Army officer)") (born 3 August 1763), the foster brother to [Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany](/wiki/Prince_Frederick,_Duke_of_York_and_Albany "Prince Frederick, Duke of York and Albany") (born 16 August 1763).[[16]](#cite_note-16)

A third son with Elisabeth Aspinwall, Francis, not identified above, was buried in Gommerville, Seine Maritime, France on 25 August 1779 aged about 3 years old. In the Parish records it states of the father "Guillaume Nevil' Hart gentilhomme anglois et Cy devant membre du parlement d'angleterre..." The mother is recorded as Elisabeth Aspinwall and their religion "religion pretendue reformee" – Protestants.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
